commits.name: Commit-0
commits.content:
Post-mortem satisfaction survey

A strong year for student research

142 participants responded to the post-event satisfaction survey completed April 23–24, 2026. Results reflect broadly positive experiences across all core dimensions, with clear and actionable paths for improvement.

Responses
142
participants surveyed
Avg. rating
4.21
out of 5
Rated 4 or 5
75%
overall experience
Faculty advisor
4.57
avg. satisfaction
No issues reported
83%
of respondents
Faculty advising was the highest-scoring dimension. Digital tools debuted to broad positive reception. The overwhelming majority of participants reported no issues — and where they did, the office resolved them at a high rate.

Core satisfaction ratings

Three dimensions rated 1–5 by participants.

Overall experience
4.21 / 5
75% rated 4–5

“I feel like there was very little communication about expectations for student involvement during the event, such as whether we were expected to present our posters or not” — Nora
“A photo of the tables set up with trifold posters would’ve been helpful to see to prepare better. There was no information beforehand about needing a trifold as well.” — Emma
“It was unclear that we would need to provide a poster board to set up our posters on until it was mentioned in an email that those on hand had run out. I would have appreciated more advance notice so I could have been better prepared.” — Livia
“Please make the research day website better. The dates, expectations, and materials need to be clearly explained. Not just vaguely and difficult to find.” — Yaran
Faculty advisor satisfaction was the highest-scoring dimension — 69 of 103 respondents gave a perfect 5. Communication showed the most spread, with a meaningful cluster of 1–2 ratings pointing to gaps in pre-event outreach.

New digital tools — first-year reception

Six tools introduced this year. Bars show share of responses per sentiment category (n ≈ 142).

SharePoint event page
70% positive · 26% neutral · 4% problematic
Editable registration form
75% positive · 21% neutral · 4% problematic
Interactive table map
67% positive · 29% neutral · 4% problematic
Digital judging
64% positive · 31% neutral · 5% problematic
Option to receive judge feedback
70% positive · 22% neutral · 8% problematic
Automated email reminders
72% positive · 22% neutral · 6% problematic
Very helpful Somewhat helpful Neutral Problematic
Judge feedback and automated reminders led the pack. None were rated as very problematic, but digital judging had the highest friction rate — worth monitoring as the workflow matures.

Judging performance snapshot

Event judging activity highlights from this cycle.

Scorecards completed
478
total submissions scored
Avg. time
04:39
per scorecard
Judges registered
85
active evaluator pool
Avg project load
1:8
projects per judge
Max project load
10
highest assigned load
Comments logged
279
written judge comments
Judges completed 478 scorecards at an average pace of 4 minutes and 39 seconds each. The 279 written comments signal strong qualitative engagement, helping students receive more actionable feedback.

Areas participants want to see grow

Participants selected all improvements they'd like for next year's event (n = 108, multiple selections allowed).

Calendar invites for times & locations
62 (57%)
Centralized print portal
51 (47%)
Modernized templates (Canva)
45 (42%)
Presentation/verbal category
36 (33%)
Additional networking time
35 (32%)
Industry collaborations
34 (31%)
Faculty involvement in judging
31 (29%)
Additional digital tools
21 (19%)
Teams for event information
21 (19%)
Interdepartmental robotics competition
15 (14%)
Calendar invites (57%) and a centralized print portal (47%) top the list — logistics pain points with clear, actionable solutions. Strong demand for a verbal presentation category (33%) signals appetite for a broader event format.

Issues reported & resolution

18 participants (17%) reported an issue. The office resolved the large majority — 14 of 18 confirmed resolved.

Printing defect
7 reports
6/7 resolved
86% resolution rate
Missed event — not aware
4 reports
3/4 resolved
75% resolution rate
Missed event — academic conflict
4 reports
2/4 resolved
50% resolution rate
Award ceremony error
1 report
1/1 resolved
100% resolution rate
Other / unspecified
2 reports
1/2 resolved
50% resolution rate
Printing defects were the most common issue and nearly all reported issues were resolved. Missed events due to lack of awareness point directly to communication gaps — calendar invites address both problems simultaneously. Scheduling conflicts are harder to resolve operationally; wider scheduling windows may help.

Looking ahead to RCIS 2027

Three priority areas emerging from participant feedback.

01
Strengthen communication logistics
  • Add calendar invites for all event times and locations
  • Clarify poster format requirements earlier with visual aids (trifold, dimensions)
  • Send explicit pre-event participant guidance
02
Reduce preventable print issues
  • Build a centralized print portal with postscript preview capability
  • Provide embedded-font and margin checklist at submission
  • Offer modernized templates on accessible platforms (Canva)
03
Build engagement and presence
  • Explore live or asynchronous verbal presentation competition category.
  • Increase networking and faculty feedback time or enhance the impact of interactions within the same timeframe;
  • Build on existing advisor relationships to solve load balancing issues for judges
  • Pursue industry partnerships for broader recognition
RCIS 2026 demonstrated strong faculty engagement, digital infrastructure, and participant enthusiasm. Addressing logistical pain points around communication and printing can drive significant gains with relatively low effort. Longer-term growth opportunities could include exploring additional event formats and building stronger industry connections.
